title: Lossy vs. Lossless Compression
concept: Autoencoder compression is lossy (callback to Unit 91) — some detail is always lost when squeezing through the bottleneck.

exercises.prompt: When you zip a big folder into a smaller file, do you always get every single bit back perfectly when you unzip it?

options.text: It depends on the compression method used

exercises.explain: Some compression methods keep every detail perfectly (lossless), while others squeeze so much that a little detail gets lost forever (lossy). Autoencoders are the second kind!

cards.title: Squeezing a Suitcase
cards.body: Remember the autoencoder's tiny bottleneck from Unit 91? Imagine packing a huge suitcase into a tiny backpack. You can fit the essentials, but some stuff has to be left behind. That's exactly what happens when an autoencoder compresses data — and it's called being 'lossy'!

cards.title: Lossy vs. Lossless
cards.body: Lossless compression shrinks data but lets you rebuild it EXACTLY, bit by bit — nothing is lost. Lossy compression shrinks data by throwing away some tiny details that are hard to notice, so what you rebuild is close but not perfectly identical to the original. Autoencoders are lossy — the bottleneck forces them to keep only the most important patterns.

cards.title: The Photocopy Analogy
cards.body: Lossless is like photocopying a document with a perfect scanner — every letter comes out exactly the same. Lossy is like describing a photo to a friend over the phone, then having them redraw it. They'll get the big picture right, but tiny details (an exact freckle, a precise shadow) might be slightly different or missing.

cards.title: Real-World Example
cards.body: A ZIP file is lossless — unzip a text document and every word is exactly as it was. An MP3 music file is lossy — it removes sound details your ears barely notice to save space. An autoencoder compressing a face photo works like the MP3: the rebuilt face looks almost the same, but tiny details (an exact hair strand, a faint wrinkle) might blur or vanish.

cards.title: Myth: Compression Never Loses Anything
cards.body: Myth: 'Compressing data always keeps everything, it just makes the file smaller.' Truth: Only lossless methods keep everything. Many powerful compressors — including autoencoders and formats like JPEG and MP3 — are lossy on purpose, trading a bit of perfect detail for a much smaller size.

cards.title: Original vs. Rebuilt
cards.body: Compare what happens when data passes through each type of compression.

left.text: Rebuilt file = 100% identical to original, every bit restored perfectly

right.text: Rebuilt file = very close but slightly blurry, some fine detail permanently lost

exercises.prompt: Why is an autoencoder's output usually considered 'lossy'?

options.text: Because the bottleneck forces it to drop some fine details
Because it deletes the input file immediately
Because it only works on images
Because it makes files bigger, not smaller

exercises.explain: The tiny bottleneck can't hold all the original information, so the autoencoder keeps only the most important patterns and loses some fine detail.

exercises.prompt: A ZIP file is an example of lossy compression.
exercises.explain: ZIP files are lossless — when you unzip them, you get back the exact original data with nothing missing.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence.
exercises.sentence: Because the autoencoder's bottleneck is so small, some detail is always ___ when data is squeezed through it.
exercises.explain: The small bottleneck can't store every detail, so some information is lost — this is why autoencoders are called 'lossy'.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the BEST way to describe lossy compression?

options.text: It keeps every single detail perfectly, with zero changes
It shrinks data by removing some details that are hard to notice, so the rebuilt version is close but not identical
It only works with sound files, never images

exercises.explain: Lossy compression trades a small, usually unnoticeable amount of detail for a much smaller file size — that's exactly what autoencoders do at their bottleneck.

exercises.prompt: An autoencoder compresses a photo of a cat through its bottleneck, then rebuilds it. The output looks almost identical to the original but the whiskers are slightly blurrier. What does this show?

options.text: The autoencoder is broken and needs to be fixed
This is normal — autoencoders are lossy, so some fine detail like whiskers can be lost during compression
The autoencoder copied the file perfectly with no loss at all
The photo was accidentally deleted and replaced with a new one

exercises.explain: Slight blurriness in fine details like whiskers is expected — it's the natural result of squeezing data through a small bottleneck. This is what makes autoencoder compression 'lossy' rather than lossless.
